other_observers: Joey Kellner, Glenn Walbek, Charlie Lawrence, Ira Sanders, Joe Roller, and others; first identified by Joey Kellner
description: the bird was a small sparrow-like bird, identified as a pipit by the sparrow-like streaked brown plumage, but with a thin bill, smaller head compared to the body, and habit of running. It was slightly larger than a Savannah Sparrow. The bill was quite thin, and fairly short, though longer and heavier than expected for American Pipit. It was mainly flesh or orange colored, with a darker culmen. The eye was dark.  The plumage was primarily a medium sandy-brown. The head was pale, especially on the face, where a lack of bold markings gave the bird a "blank" faced look. The crown and nape were lighly streaked. The back was boldly streaked with very dark brown or black on the lighter sandy-brown. The bird had faint white wingbars. The tail was fairly short (for a pipit), and the outer two or three tail feathers were white, only visible in flight, with the inner tail feathers being brown. I did not see the underparts in the field.Behaviors: The bird, when flushed, would rise into the air rapidly, fly a distance, and drop like a stone onto the ground. When landed it would run a short distance, and remain absolutely still in a clump of grass, almost invisible.Call: In flight the bird was heard to give a squeeky "squeet" call a couple of times.Plumage: probable adult
photographer: Photographer: Andrew Spencer, Glenn Walbek, Glenn Walbek
